true
false

challenges.description:
Use a for loop over 1..=10 and print only the odd numbers, each on its own line. Expected output:

7

challenges.title: Above Threshold
challenges.description:
Write `count_above(nums: &[i32], threshold: i32) -> usize` that returns how many elements are strictly greater than `threshold`.

Example: `count_above(&[3, 7, 1, 9, 4], 4)` returns `2` (7 and 9).

Expected output:
```
2
```
challenges.hint: Use iter().filter() with a closure that compares each element to the threshold, then call .count().

steps.title: Understand the goal
steps.explanation: We need to count how many elements in a slice are strictly greater than a given threshold value.

steps.title: Iterate and filter
steps.explanation: Use iter() to get references to each element, then filter() with a closure. Since iter() yields &&i32, we double-dereference with |&&x|.
steps.code: nums.iter().filter(|&&x| x > threshold)

steps.title: Count the matches
steps.explanation: Chain .count() to get the number of elements that passed the filter.

challenges.title: Cap First
challenges.description:
Write `capitalize_first(word: &str) -> String` that capitalizes the first letter of a word, leaving the rest unchanged.

Example: `capitalize_first("hello")` returns `"Hello"`.

Expected output:
```
Hello
```
challenges.hint: Use chars() to get a mutable iterator. Call next() for the first character, uppercase it with to_uppercase(), then append chars.as_str() for the rest.

steps.title: Extract the first character
steps.explanation: Use chars() to get an iterator over Unicode characters, then call next() to get the first one as an Option<char>.
steps.code:
let mut chars = word.chars();
let first = chars.next();

steps.title: Handle empty string with match
steps.explanation: Pattern match on the Option. If None, return an empty String. If Some(c), uppercase the character and append the rest.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: The main function calls capitalize_first("hello") and prints the result, which should be "Hello".

challenges.title: Sort Check
challenges.description:
Write `is_sorted(nums: &[i32]) -> bool` that returns true if the slice is sorted in non-decreasing order.

Example: `is_sorted(&[1, 2, 2, 5])` returns `true`, `is_sorted(&[3, 1, 2])` returns `false`.

Expected output:
```
true
false
```
challenges.hint: Use the windows(2) method on slices to compare each pair of consecutive elements.

steps.title: Understand sorted order
steps.explanation: A slice is sorted in non-decreasing order if every element is less than or equal to the next one.

steps.title: Use windows for pairwise comparison
steps.explanation: The windows(2) method yields overlapping pairs [w[0], w[1]]. The all() adapter checks that every pair satisfies w[0] <= w[1].

steps.title: Verify with test cases
steps.explanation: For [1,2,2,5] every pair is non-decreasing so it returns true. For [3,1,2] the pair [3,1] fails so it returns false.

challenges.title: Mean Value
challenges.description:
Write `average(nums: &[i32]) -> f64` that returns the arithmetic mean of the elements. Assume the slice is non-empty.

Example: `average(&[10, 20, 30])` returns `20.0`.

Expected output:
```
20
```
challenges.hint: Sum the elements with iter().sum::<i32>(), cast to f64, then divide by the length as f64.

steps.title: Sum the elements
steps.explanation: Use iter().sum::<i32>() to compute the total. The turbofish ::<i32> tells Rust what type to sum into.
steps.code: let sum: i32 = nums.iter().sum();

steps.title: Compute the mean
steps.explanation: Cast both the sum and the length to f64 before dividing to get a floating-point result.
steps.code: sum as f64 / nums.len() as f64

steps.title: Verify the output
steps.explanation: For [10, 20, 30], sum is 60 and length is 3, so 60.0 / 3.0 = 20.0. Rust prints this as 20.

challenges.title: Unique Only
challenges.description:
Write `remove_duplicates(nums: Vec<i32>) -> Vec<i32>` that removes duplicate values, keeping the first occurrence, preserving order.

Example: `remove_duplicates(vec![1, 2, 2, 3, 1, 4])` returns `[1, 2, 3, 4]`.

Expected output:
```
[1, 2, 3, 4]
```
challenges.hint: Create a Vec and a HashSet. Iterate over nums; if the set doesn't contain the value, insert it into both the set and the result vec.

steps.title: Track seen values
steps.explanation: Use a HashSet to efficiently track which values we have already encountered. HashSet::insert returns true if the value was newly inserted.
steps.code: let mut seen = HashSet::new();

steps.title: Build the result
steps.explanation: Iterate over the input. For each element, try to insert into the set. If insert returns true (new value), push it onto the result vector.

steps.title: Verify order is preserved
steps.explanation: Since we iterate in order and only add new values, the first occurrence order is preserved. Output: [1, 2, 3, 4].

challenges.title: String Joiner
challenges.description:
Write `join_words(words: &[&str], sep: &str) -> String` that joins the words with the given separator.

Example: `join_words(&["rust", "is", "fun"], "-")` returns `"rust-is-fun"`.

Expected output:
```
rust-is-fun
```
challenges.hint: You can collect the slice into a Vec<&str> (it already is one) and use the .join(sep) method on slices of strings.

steps.title: Understand join
steps.explanation: Rust slices of strings (&[&str]) have a built-in .join(separator) method that concatenates elements with the separator between them.

steps.title: Implement the function
steps.explanation: Simply call words.join(sep) which returns a new String with all words connected by the separator.

steps.title: Verify the output
steps.explanation: For ["rust", "is", "fun"] with separator "-", the output is "rust-is-fun".

challenges.title: Struct Method
challenges.description:
Define a struct `Rectangle` with fields `width: f64` and `height: f64`. Implement a method `area(&self) -> f64` that returns the area.

Expected output:
```
15
```
challenges.hint: Multiply self.width by self.height to get the area.

steps.title: Define the struct
steps.explanation: A struct in Rust groups related data. Rectangle has two f64 fields: width and height.

steps.title: Implement the method
steps.explanation: Use an impl block to define methods. The &self parameter borrows the struct. Multiply the fields to get the area.

steps.title: Verify the result
steps.explanation: 3.0 * 5.0 = 15.0, which Rust prints as 15.

challenges.title: Silver Medal
challenges.description:
Write `second_largest(nums: &[i32]) -> Option<i32>` that returns the second largest value, or None if it doesn't exist. Assume values may repeat.

Example: `second_largest(&[5, 3, 9, 9, 1])` returns `Some(5)`.

Expected output:
```
5
```
challenges.hint: Sort a mutable copy in descending order, deduplicate it, then return the second element if it exists.

steps.title: Clone and sort
steps.explanation: Convert the slice to a Vec with to_vec(), then sort it. sort_unstable() is faster and works fine when we don't need stability.
steps.code:
let mut sorted = nums.to_vec();
sorted.sort_unstable();

steps.title: Remove duplicates
steps.explanation: dedup() removes consecutive duplicates from a sorted vec, giving us unique values in ascending order.
steps.code: sorted.dedup();

steps.title: Return the second largest
steps.explanation: After sorting ascending and deduplicating, the second largest is at index len()-2. Return None if fewer than 2 unique values.

challenges.title: Char Count
challenges.description:
Write `count_char(text: &str, target: char) -> usize` that counts how many times `target` appears in `text`.

Example: `count_char("abracadabra", 'a')` returns `5`.

Expected output:
```
5
```
challenges.hint: Use chars().filter() to keep only characters equal to target, then call .count().

steps.title: Iterate over characters
steps.explanation: Use chars() to get an iterator over each Unicode character in the string.
steps.code: text.chars()

steps.title: Filter and count
steps.explanation: Chain filter(|&c| c == target) to keep only matching characters, then .count() to get the total.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: In "abracadabra", the letter 'a' appears at positions 0, 3, 5, 7, 10 — five times total.

challenges.title: FizzBuzz Run
challenges.description:
Write `fizzbuzz(n: u32) -> String` that returns:
- `"FizzBuzz"` if n is divisible by both 3 and 5
- `"Fizz"` if divisible by 3 only
- `"Buzz"` if divisible by 5 only
- the number as a string otherwise.

Print the result for 1..=15, each on its own line.

Expected output:
```
1
2
Fizz
4
Buzz
Fizz
7
8
Fizz
Buzz
11
Fizz
13
14
FizzBuzz
```
challenges.hint: Use match with a tuple (n % 3, n % 5). Match (0, 0) for FizzBuzz, (0, _) for Fizz, (_, 0) for Buzz, and _ for the number.

steps.title: Understand FizzBuzz rules
steps.explanation: FizzBuzz is a classic problem: replace multiples of 3 with Fizz, multiples of 5 with Buzz, and multiples of both with FizzBuzz.

steps.title: Use tuple matching
steps.explanation: Create a tuple (n % 3, n % 5) and match it. This elegantly handles all four cases without nested ifs.

steps.title: Loop and print
steps.explanation: Use a range 1..=15 to iterate and print each result. The ..= syntax creates an inclusive range.

challenges.title: Palindrome Test
challenges.description:
Write `is_palindrome(s: &str) -> bool` that checks if a string reads the same forwards and backwards (case-sensitive).

Example: `is_palindrome("racecar")` returns `true`, `is_palindrome("hello")` returns `false`.

Expected output:
```
true
false
```
challenges.hint: Compare the original chars iterator with its reversed version using eq().

steps.title: Collect characters
steps.explanation: Convert the string to a Vec<char> so we can easily compare it with its reverse.
steps.code: let chars: Vec<char> = s.chars().collect();

steps.title: Compare with reverse
steps.explanation: Reverse the vector and compare. If they are equal, the string is a palindrome.
steps.code: chars == chars.iter().copied().rev().collect::<Vec<char>>()

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: "racecar" reversed is "racecar" (same), so true. "hello" reversed is "olleh" (different), so false.

challenges.title: Flat Merge
challenges.description:
Write `flatten(nested: Vec<Vec<i32>>) -> Vec<i32>` that flattens a vector of vectors into a single vector.

Example: `flatten(vec![vec![1, 2], vec![3], vec![4, 5, 6]])` returns `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]`.

Expected output:
```
[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
```
challenges.hint: Use into_iter().flatten().collect() to flatten the nested iterators into a single collection.

steps.title: Understand flatten
steps.explanation: The flatten() adapter on iterators takes an iterator of iterables and yields all inner elements sequentially.

steps.title: Chain the operations
steps.explanation: Use into_iter() to consume the outer Vec, flatten() to merge inner Vecs, and collect() to gather results.

steps.title: Verify the output
steps.explanation: The three inner vectors [1,2], [3], [4,5,6] are merged into [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6].

challenges.title: Square Map
challenges.description:
Write `square_all(nums: &[i32]) -> Vec<i32>` that returns a new vector where each element is the square of the original.

Example: `square_all(&[1, 2, 3, 4])` returns `[1, 4, 9, 16]`.

Expected output:
```
[1, 4, 9, 16]
```
challenges.hint: Use iter().map() with a closure that squares each element, then collect() into a Vec.

steps.title: Use map to transform
steps.explanation: The map() adapter applies a closure to each element. |&x| x * x dereferences and squares each value.
steps.code: nums.iter().map(|&x| x * x)

steps.title: Collect the results
steps.explanation: Chain collect() to gather the mapped values into a new Vec<i32>.

challenges.title: Word Frequency
challenges.description:
Write `word_freq(text: &str) -> std::collections::HashMap<String, usize>` that counts how often each word appears. Words are split by whitespace.

Print the count of "the" in the sentence: `"the cat and the dog and the fish"`.

Expected output:
```
3
```
challenges.hint: Split the text by whitespace, iterate, and use the entry API with or_insert(0) to increment counts.

steps.title: Split into words
steps.explanation: Use split_whitespace() to get an iterator over words, which handles multiple spaces and trims edges.
steps.code: text.split_whitespace()

steps.title: Count with entry API
steps.explanation: The entry() API on HashMap provides an efficient way to insert-or-update. or_insert(0) gives a mutable reference to the count, which we increment with +=1.

steps.title: Look up a specific word
steps.explanation: Use map.get("the") which returns Option<&usize>. unwrap_or(&0) provides a default if the key is missing.

challenges.title: Enum Display
challenges.description:
Define an enum `Color` with variants `Red`, `Green`, and `Blue`. Implement a method `name(&self) -> &str` that returns the color as a string.

Print each color name on its own line.

Expected output:
```
Red
Green
Blue
```
challenges.hint: Use match on self to return a string slice for each variant: Self::Red => "Red", etc.

steps.title: Define the enum
steps.explanation: Rust enums define a type with a fixed set of variants. Each variant is like a named constant.

steps.title: Match on self
steps.explanation: In the impl block, use match self to return a different string slice for each variant. Rust enforces exhaustive matching.

steps.title: Iterate and print
steps.explanation: Create an array of all variants and loop with for c in &colors to borrow each one, calling c.name() to print.

challenges.title: Word Counter
challenges.description: Write `word_count(text: &str) -> std::collections::HashMap<String, usize>` counting whitespace-separated words. Example: `"go rust go" -> {"go":2,"rust":1}`.
challenges.hint: Use entry(...).or_insert(0) += 1.

steps.explanation: Read the requirement carefully and focus on producing the exact expected output or return value.

steps.explanation: Apply the language feature that directly solves the task.

steps.title: Verify expected result
steps.explanation: Run the code with the example case and confirm the output/return value matches exactly.

challenges.title: Run Totals
challenges.description: Write `running_sum(numbers: &[i32]) -> Vec<i32>` returning cumulative sums. Example: `[2,1,3] -> [2,3,6]`.
challenges.hint: Track mutable total and push after each addition.

challenges.title: Prime Guard
challenges.description: Write `is_prime(n: u64) -> bool` returning true for primes and false otherwise; 0 and 1 are not prime.
challenges.hint: Check divisors while d*d <= n.

challenges.title: Rotate Right
challenges.description: Write `rotate_right(items: &[i32], k: usize) -> Vec<i32>` rotating right by k. Example: `([1,2,3,4],1) -> [4,1,2,3]`.
challenges.hint: Use modulo shift and combine two slices.

challenges.description: Write `merge_intervals(intervals: &[[i32; 2]]) -> Vec<[i32; 2]>` merging overlaps. Example: `[[1,3],[2,6],[8,10]] -> [[1,6],[8,10]]`.
challenges.hint: Sort by start then merge against the last output interval.

challenges.title: Top K Words
challenges.description: Write `top_k_frequent(words: &[&str], k: usize) -> Vec<String>` returning top-k by frequency, lexicographic tiebreak. Example: `["i","love","i","code"],2 -> ["i","code"]`.
challenges.hint: Count with HashMap then sort entries by count desc and word asc.

challenges.title: Deep Flatten
challenges.description: Define enum `Nested { Num(i32), List(Vec<Nested>) }` and write `flatten_deep(items: &[Nested]) -> Vec<i32>` returning depth-first flattened numbers. Example: `[Num(1), List(vec![Num(2), List(vec![Num(3)])])] -> [1,2,3]`.
challenges.hint: Use recursion to walk nested lists.
